coredata.dc:title: Graphene quantum dots modified nanoporous SiAl composite as an advanced anode for lithium storage
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Zizhong
Hou, Jiagang
Liu, Qiang
Zhou, Qiuxia
Liu, Hong
Xu, Caixia
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots modified nanoporous SiAl (NP-SiAl/GQDs) composite was easily prepared by a facile dealloying followed by the encapsulation of graphene quantum dots. Nanoporous Si with part of Al residues was first fabricated through incomplete corrosion of binary Si5Al95 precursor alloy. GQDs were then used as the building units to enwrap the positively charged NP-SiAl on the basis of the electrostatic assembly. Owing to the combination of graphene quantum dots and embedded Al, NP-SiAl/GQDs displays dramatically boosted lithium storage performances with excellent reversible capacity, outstanding cyclability, as well as high rate capability. Especially, NP-SiAl/GQDs composite exhibits superior cycling stability with large reversible capacity of 1318.7 and 1005.5 mAh g−1 retained after 120 and 150 cycles under the rate of 200 and 1000 mA g−1, respectively. Moreover, it exhibits high reversible capacity even cycled at 3000 mA g−1. With the merits of facile preparation and exceptional performances, the as-prepared NP-SiAl/GQDs composite presents great application potential as next-generation anode material in lithium ion batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nanoporous
SiAl
Graphene quantum dots
Anode
Lithium ion batteries

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 51772133 ), Shandong Province ( ZR2017JL022 ), the program for Taishan Scholar of Shandong province ( ts201712048 ), and China postdoctoral science foundation ( 2017M622117 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: SEM images of the samples upon etching Si5Al95 alloy in 0.05 M NaOH for 10 h (a) and after combination with GQDs (b), (c, d) TEM and HRTEM images of the NP-SiAl/GQDs composite.

(a) XRD patterns of the Si5Al95 precursor alloy, dealloyed sample, and the final NP-SiAl/GQDs with the standard patterns of Si (JCPDS 65-1060) and Al (JCPDS 65-2869) for comparison. (b) Raman spectra of NP-SiAl, GQDs, and NP-SiAl/GQDs composite.
XPS survey scans of NP-SiAl/GQDs composite (a), Si 2p peaks (b), C 1s peaks (c), and Al 2p peaks (d).
(a) CV curves of the NP-SiAl/GQDs anode. (b) The first three cycles for galvanostatic charge/discharge profiles of the NP-SiAl/GQDs anode at 200 mA g−1.
(a, c) Cycling performances and (b, d) coulombic efficiencies of NP-SiAl and NP-SiAl/GQDs electrodes.
(a) Rate performances, and (b) impedance measurements of the NP-SiAl and NP-SiAl/GQDs electrodes.

head.ce:title: Graphene quantum dots modified nanoporous SiAl composite as an advanced anode for lithium storage

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute for Advanced Interdisciplinary Research (iAIR), Collaborative Innovation Center of Technology and Equipment for Biological Diagnosis and Therapy in Universities of Shandong, University of Jinan, Jinan, 250022, Shandong Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Technology and Equipment for Biological Diagnosis and Therapy in Universities of Shandong

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan, 250353, Shandong Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Qilu University of Technology (Shandong Academy of Sciences), Jinan, 250353, Shandong Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, Jinan, 250100, Shandong Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Crystal Materials, Shandong University, , 250100, Shandong Province, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots modified nanoporous SiAl (NP-SiAl/GQDs) composite was easily prepared by a facile dealloying followed by the encapsulation of graphene quantum dots. Nanoporous Si with part of Al residues was first fabricated through incomplete corrosion of binary Si5Al95 precursor alloy. GQDs were then used as the building units to enwrap the positively charged NP-SiAl on the basis of the electrostatic assembly. Owing to the combination of graphene quantum dots and embedded Al, NP-SiAl/GQDs displays dramatically boosted lithium storage performances with excellent reversible capacity, outstanding cyclability, as well as high rate capability. Especially, NP-SiAl/GQDs composite exhibits superior cycling stability with large reversible capacity of 1318.7 and 1005.5 mAh g−1 retained after 120 and 150 cycles under the rate of 200 and 1000 mA g−1, respectively. Moreover, it exhibits high reversible capacity even cycled at 3000 mA g−1. With the merits of facile preparation and exceptional performances, the as-prepared NP-SiAl/GQDs composite presents great application potential as next-generation anode material in lithium ion batteries.

A cotton-like protective layer formed on the surface of NP-SiAl backbone was conveniently fabricated through selective dealloying of Si5Al95 binary alloy under mild conditions followed by a surface combination with graphene quantum dots. The as-prepared NP-SiAl/GQDs composite is composed of bimodal SiAl network skeleton and interconnected graphene quantum dots protective layer uniformly distributed on the surface. With the advantages of unique structure and excellent lithium storage performances, NP-SiAl/GQDs composite holds promising application potential as an anode material in LIBs.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Lithium ion batteries (LIBs) have became critical energy storage devices for a variety of applications including portable electronic devices, electric vehicles, and grid storage due to its lack of memory effects, relatively high energy density, and good rate capability [1–3]. Because of the relatively lower theoretical capacity (372 mAh g−1), commercially used graphite anode cannot satisfy the requirements for the high power and energy densities of LIBs. Therefore, exploring alternative anode materials with high specific capacity are of important significance for the development of next-generation LIBs with long cycling life, good safety, and high energy density.

Among all the candidates, silicon (Si) is considered as the most promising anode for replacing graphite owing to the following superiorities: (1) high theoretical capacity (4200 mAh g−1) and volumetric capacity (9786 mAh cm−3, calculated under 25 °C); (2) proper discharge voltage (ca. 0.5 V), which can eliminate adverse lithium plating process and maintain a reasonable open circuit voltage; (3) high abundance, non-toxicity, environment friendliness, and low cost [4]. However, huge structure stress and volume expansion (around 300% for Li4.4Si) are accompanied for Si anode during the repeated lithiation/delithiation process [5,6]. The huge volume expansion usually causes a chain of severe destructive consequences. For example, electrode structure is destroyed due to gradual pulverization during constant charge/discharge processes, leading to the active material fall off from the current collector. Furthermore, successive consumption of electrolyte during the continuous formation-breaking-reformation process of solid electrolyte interphase (SEI) layer. These processes quicken the capacity fading and structure collapse for Si electrode in a synergistic way. In addition, the poor electron conductivity of Si results in the sluggish electrochemical kinetics [7]. For addressing the aforementioned issues, tremendous efforts have been dedicated since the last century. Some strategies have been developed including the utilization of nanoscale silicon and the construction of physical compartment to accommodate the volume expansion [8–10].

In recent years, various Si nanomaterials have been developed to alleviate the structure pulverization for improve the lithium storage performance, such as nanotube [11], nanowire [12], hollow structure [13], and porous structures [14–16]. In addition, it has been demonstrated that coupling with carbon materials, such as amorphous carbon [17,18], carbon nanotubes [19], graphene [20,21], and reduced graphene oxide [22–24] can efficiently optimize the electric connectivity, cushion the structure fracture, as well as impede the side reactions for Si anodes. As a new kind of carbon material, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have attracted wide attentions and widely used in supercapacitors [25] and battery [26–28] due to their low toxicity, high stability, and hydrophilic nature [29]. For example, Niu et al. have found that graphene quantum dots can act as a novel conductive additive to improve the capacitive performance for supercapacitors [25]. Chao et al. designed a new type of binder-free cathode by bottom-up growth of biface VO2 arrays directly on a graphene network for both high performance Li-ion and Na-ion battery cathodes [28]. In recent years, Li et al. successfully prepared a series of functionalized graphene quantum dots for improving the electron transfer and the lithium storage capacity [30–32]. Li et al. significantly enhanced the electrochemical performances of lithium titanate anode for lithium ion battery by introducing the nitrogen and sulfur co-doped graphene quantum dots [32]. Moreover, alloying Si with the conductive metals, such as Cu, Ag, Ni, Ti, represents another popular strategy to improve the conductivity and thus the lithium storage performances of Si [33–38].

In this work, we aimed to enhance the lithium storage performances of Si by way of the construction of porous nanostructure as well as the introduction of highly conductive Al and graphene quantum dots. GQDs modified NP-SiAl composite was easily prepared by combining dealloying strategy with the encapsulation process. First, binary Si5Al95 precursor alloy was incompletely etched to obtain nanoporous Si with part of Al residues as the conductive hybrid. Second, GQDs were used as the building units to enwrap the positively charged NP-SiAl by the electrostatic assembly. It is notable that the as-prepared NP-SiAl/GQDs composite exhibits the outstanding lithium storage performances owing to the following advantages. First, intercrossed network architecture can ensure a continuous pathway for the transmission of electrons and ions. Second, the in-situ encapsulation by GQDs outside of the nanoporous surface serve as a bifunctional layer, which not only deliver an excellent conductive for Si but also accommodate the volume change during Li alloying/dealloying processes. Consequently, the NP-SiAl/GQDs composite realizes a superior lithium storage capacity and high rate capacity. Our findings demonstrate a fascinating Si-based anode for the potential application in the next-generation LIBs.

A simple bottom-up method was adopted for prepare GQDs through tuning the carbonization degree of citric acid (CA) [29]. In a typical preparation of GQDs, 2.5 g CA is putted into a beaker and heated to 200 °C by using oil bath. The CA liquated after few minutes. And the colour of liquid changed from achromatic colour to pale yellow. Finally, the colour became orange, indicating the formation of GQDs. And then, the orange liquid was dropped into 100 mL of 10 mg mL−1 NaOH solution with vigorous stirring until neutralized to pH = 7.0.

Pure Si and Al (purity, 99.99 wt %) were melted by using an Ar-filled electric resistance furnace to form Si5Al95 ingot. The ingot was further rapidly solidified into foils by using a single roller melt spinning apparatus at 1200 r/min with Ar atmosphere as protective gas. As-prepared Si5Al95 binary alloy foils were selectively etched in 0.05 M NaOH solution at room temperature for 10 h. Then, the sample was collected by filter and washed with deionized (DI) water by three times followed drying at 55 °C.

In the typical synthesis, 0.5 g NP-SiAl alloy was sonicated in 50 mL absolute ethyl alcohol for form a homogeneous suspension. And then, 0.5 mL aminopropyltrimethoxysilane (APS) was added into the homogeneous suspension under sonicating for 0.5 h and stirring 12 h. The sample was collected by centrifugation, washed with absolute ethyl alcohol, and dried at 55 °C for overnight. Eventually, 50 mg APS-modified NP-SiAl were added into 30 ml GQDs solution with vigorous stirring for 10 h. The resulting sample was collected by centrifugation, washed with DI water and absolute ethyl alcohol by three times, respectively, and dried at 60 °C. The product was named as NP-SiAl/GQDs.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5418 Å) was used to identify the crystal structure of the product with a scan rate of 0.04° per second from 20 to 80°. A JEOL JSM-6700F field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM) equipped with Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) was utilized for observe the microstructures and compositional analysis of the made sample under 200 kV. Low- and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was employed for further characterize the as-prepared products. The surface chemical composition of the samples was verified by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (250 XI). Raman spectra were recorded by using a laser Raman spectrometer (Raman, Horiba) under excitation wavelength of 530 nm.

The active materials, acetylene black, and sodium alginate in a ratio of 6: 2.5: 1.5 (wt. %) were mixed to form the slurry by using the DI water as solvent. The slurry was casted on a current collector (Cu foil) and dried at 55 °C for 12 h. The as-made Cu foil was punched into circular piece with a diameter of 1.0 cm. The active material on the circular piece was accounted through difference subtraction. The gravimetric density of the used electrode materials is around 0.54 mg cm−2. Coin-cells (CR2032) were assembled in an Ar-filled glove box and controlled H2O and O2 contents < 0.1 ppm. The Li foil and Celgard-2300 membrane were utilized as counter electrode and separator, respectively. The electrolyte was composed of 1 M LiPF6 dissolved in dimethyl carbonate, ethylene carbonate, and ethylene methyl carbonate (v/v/v = 1:1:1). Galvanostatic charge-discharge tests were carried out by using NEWARE BTS system (Shenzhen, China) between 0.01 and 1.5 V. The electrochemical performances of the fresh cells were explored at various current densities under the constant temperature. The cyclic voltammetry measurement was carried out on CHI 760 E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai CH Instruments Co., China) with a scan rate of 0.2 mV s−1 in range of 0.01–1.5 V. Electrochemistry impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was tested by using Princeton Applied Research spectrometer with an alternating voltage of 10 mV in the frequency range from 0.01 to 100 kHz.

It has been well documented that uniform porous nanoscale architecture can be created easily by selective etching of more active element from the well-designed precursor alloy [36–38]. In this work, we first adopt one simple, low-cost, and safety dealloying route to construct high porosity and nanoscale structure for Si. Nanoporous Si with part of conductive Al residues (denoted as NP-SiAl) was prepared through incomplete corrosion of binary Si5Al95 precursor alloy. It is known that the as-made GQDs in solution were negatively charged [29]. Thus, the nanoporous surface for NP-SiAl was first modified by APS grafting to carry rich NH2 groups [39]. The negatively charged GQDs were adsorbed on the APS-modified NP-SiAl due to the electrostatic interactions. GQDs were used as the building units to enwrap the positively charged NP-SiAl to fabricate NP-SiAl/GQDs composite material. Under optimal conditions, almost all the added GQDs can be mounted on the porous surface based on the electrostatic assembly.

SEM and TEM were first used to characterize the microstructures of the as-made samples. From Fig. 1 a, it can be clearly found that the porous network nanostructure with high porosity and interconnected nanowire-like ligaments was generated after selectively removing Al atoms from Si5Al95 precursor alloy. The content of residual Al can be well controlled by tuning the corrosion time. After the modification of GQDs, the initial porous morphology was well maintained (Fig. 1b). The as-prepared product was further characterized by TEM for observe the detailed structure after modification with GQDs. In Fig. 1c, the interlinking dark skeletons and inner bright regions further confirm the well preservation of nanoporous architecture upon GQDs cladding. It is evident that one flocculent protective layer formed on the porous surface of SiAl backbone. Moreover, from the high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image (Fig. 1d), one lattice spacing at ca. 0.21 nm can be assigned to the (100) planes of graphene (highlighted by blue cycles) [40,41]. In addition, the highly ordered lattice fringes with the spacing of 0.14 and 0.31 nm are ascribed to the (220) crystal planes of Al [42] and (111) crystal planes of Si structure [43], respectively. It is clear that there are lots of crystallized GQDs distributed on the ligament surface, indicating the formation of GQDs enwrapped porous SiAl composites. Furthermore, the constituents of the Si5Al95 precursor alloy and the etched samples were confirmed by EDS. We can see from Fig. S1, the component of binary alloy is Si4.86Al95.14 (at. %), which is well consistent with the initial feeding. Upon exploring the effects of corrosion time on the resulting Al contents, a period of 10 h is optimized to obtain the atomic ratio of Si and Al around 77.56: 22.48 in the dealloyed sample.

XRD was utilized to analyze the crystal structures of Si5Al95 alloy, NP-SiAl, and NP-SiAl/GQDs composite. As shown in Fig. 2 a, it is observed that a set of sharp diffraction peaks located at 28.5, 47.2, 56.1, 69.3, and 76.3° (2θ) correspond well to the diamond cubic structure of Si. Meanwhile, the diffraction peaks of 2θ around 38.6, 44.8, 65.2, and 78.4° can be assigned to the standard Al phase. After Si5Al95 alloy was dealloyed in NaOH solution, the diffraction peaks of Al dramatically weakened in the as-made sample. It showed that most Al atoms in Si5Al95 binary alloy have been leached out in NaOH solution. Therefore, from the XRD analysis, we can draw a conclusion that a part of Al atoms were retained in the composite. After further combined the dealloyed sample with GQDs, the intensity and position of the diffraction peaks have no change compared with those of NP-SiAl. In order to further confirm the modification of GQDs on NP-SiAl, the Raman spectra was utilized to examine the existence of GQDs. Fig. 2b shows the Raman spectra of the GQDs, NP-SiAl, and NP-SiAl/GQDs composite. The data were recorded under the same condition without any further processing. For the individual GQDs, two absorbance bands located at around 1337.7 and 1572.7 cm−1 belong to the sp3-hybridized carbon (D band) and sp2-hybridized graphitic domains (G band) of carbon, respectively [29]. The specific diffraction peak at 506.7 cm−1 for NP-SiAl can be assigned to the first-order optical phonon of Si. Besides, the peaks at ca. 297.6 and 927.3 cm−1 are owing to the scatterings of two transverse acoustic and optical phonons for Si, respectively [17,20]. The typical diffraction peaks of Si and carbon all emerged in NP-SiAl/GQDs, indicating the successful modification of GQDs on NP-SiAl. Moreover, the value of ID/IG is only 0.988, implying a high degree of well-ordered graphite [44], which is beneficial for providing good electrical conductivity for Si anode.

The resulting NP-SiAl/GQDs sample was further characterized by XPS. From the full scan shown in Fig. 3 a, the final product is mainly composed of O, Si, C, and Al elements. As presented in Fig. 3b, the Si 2p spectra in NP-SiAl/GQDs composite display the existence of Si and SiOx. The peaks around at 98.3 and 102.2 eV can be attributed to Si and SiOx, respectively [45]. Si oxides formed because that the fresh Si atoms easily combined with oxygen during the drying procedure. Fig. 3c displays the data of C 1s for NP-SiAl/GQDs. The peak around 284.8 eV corresponds to the C-C, while the peak at 288.8 eV can be assigned to the C=O. In the Al 2p region (Fig. 3d) the peak centered at 72.9 eV is attributed to metallic aluminum [46]. From the XPS results, we can draw the conclusion that the metallic Al and GQDs have been incorporated into the nanoporous Si skeleton. Thus, based on the experimental results above, the target NP-SiAl/GQDs was fabricated successfully through etching of Si5Al95 source alloy and the encapsulation with GQDs.

The well-distributed and interconnected GQDs on the porous surface can not only play good protective role for the porous SiAl skeleton but also serve as the robust conductive network during the lithium storage process. It is vital to investigate its lithiation/delithiation properties of NP-SiAl/GQDs composite by cycle voltamemetric (CV) and galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements toward the lithium storage. The lithiation and delithiation behaviors for NP-SiAl/GQDs electrode was first tested in a button half-cell at the voltage range of 0.01–1.5V with a scan rate of 0.2 mV s−1. The strong peaks in the first cycle between 0.01 and 0.18 V (Fig. 4 a) are related to the lithiation of crystalline silicon and formation of LixSi [47]. Two main oxidation peaks around 0.32 and 0.55 V can be ascribed to the formation of amorphous Si and delithiation of LixSi [21]. These peaks in the subsequent cycles gradually became stronger and broader than those in the first cycle owing to a slow activation process of the electrode. As shown in Fig. S2a, the CV curve of the NP-SiAl also displays the similar behaviors. The redox peaks were well consistent with previous reports, similar to the typical features of Si [21,47]. Fig. 4b gives the voltage profiles of the NP-SiAl/GQDs electrode for the first three cycles at 200 mA g−1 between 0.01 and 1.5 V. The first cycle exhibited a long flat plateau at 0.08 V, which can be assigned to the lithiation of crystalline Si. Subsequently, the crystal Si changed to amorphous Si, meanwhile the typical charge/discharge profiles of amorphous Si emerged [48]. Furthermore, the NP-SiAl/GQDs delivered the initial discharge capacity of 2507.1 mAh g−1 and charge capacity of 2229.5 mAh g−1 with a coulombic efficiency (CE) of 88.9%. The relatively low initial CE probably resulted from the irreversible capacity loss, including the decomposition and consumption of nonaqueous electrolyte, which is irreversible to form SEI. In the capacity-voltage plot (Fig. 4b), the good overlap of the curves after the first cycle also proves the excellent capacity reversibility of NP-SiAl/GQDs anode. The main charge and discharge voltage plateaus for NP-SiAl were similar to that of NP-SiAl/GQDs as shown in Fig. S2b. For comparison, the NP-SiAl delivered a discharge capacity of 1473.2 mAh g−1 and a reversible capacity of 1191 mAh g−1, corresponding to a CE of 80.8%. It is clearly testified that the initial CE was enhanced upon the modification of GQDs on NP-SiAl. Moreover, the reversible capacity of NP-SiAl/GQDs is about twice that of the NP-SiAl. Besides, according to the second and third curves of NP-SiAl/GQDs, its CEs were 95.5% and 96.6%, respectively, which become stable in the subsequent cycles. The second and third curves overlapped very well, proving that NP-SiAl/GQDs composite has fairly high capacity reversibility.

We further compared the cycling stabilities of NP-SiAl and NP-SiAl/GQDs anodes in view of demonstrating the effects of graphene quantum dots on the electrochemical performances. Galvanostatic charge-discharge test was performed to evaluate their cycling life in the range of 0.01–1.5 V versus Li/Li+ at the current density of 200 mA g−1 (Fig. 5 a). The NP-SiAl/GQDs achieved much higher initial capacity of 2507.1 mAh g−1 and a discharge capacity of 1318.7 mAh g−1 after 120 cycles with the capacity retention of 52.6%. As can be seen, the NP-SiAl displayed a first discharge capacity of 1473.3 mAh g−1, and the capacity quickly declined to 503.4 mAh g−1 at 120th cycle with the capacity retention of 34.2%. The boosted cycling stability can be attributed to the fact that the GQDs buffers the large volume change better, just as a thicker cushion can bear a larger structure stress. With the current density increases to 1000 mA g−1, the NP-SiAl/GQDs sample still delivers stable cycling performance with a reversible capacity of 1005.5 mAh g−1 after 150 cycles with the capacity retention of 56.3% (Fig. 5b). The NP-SiAl/GQDs composite also displays a better electrochemical stability at the high current density. It is conclusive that the NP-SiAl/GQDs composite shows superior cycling stability and much higher reversible capacity compared with those of NP-SiAl. The lower capacity for NP-SiAl in the initial cycles can be ascribed to its poor conductivity, leading to a part of Si atoms were not fully discharged. By compared with the NP-SiAl, the dramatically boosted capacity retention and CE of NP-SiAl/GQDs composite can be assigned to the encapsulation of GQDs, which can protect the Si from direct contact with electrolyte, thus reducing the loss of the irreversible capacity during each cycle.

In addition, high rate capacity is another crucial indispensable parameter for an advanced anode. Fig. 6 a provides the rate capacities of NP-SiAl and NP-SiAl/GQDs anodes evaluated at 100, 300, 500, 1000, 2000, and 3000 mA g−1. The NP-SiAl/GQDs composite achieved the average specific discharge capacities of 2130.4, 1706.4, 1553.0, 1268.3, 990.1, and 749.3 mAh g−1. Notably, the capacity can perfectly recover to 1916.4 mAh g−1 when the current density restored to 100 mA g−1. Under the same current density, the NP-SiAl electrode exhibits a much poor rate capability. It is obvious that the NP-SiAl/GQDs exhibited superior rate performances compared with the NP-SiAl. The good rate performances of the NP-SiAl/GQDs composite are considered to result from fluent ion and electron transfer via the protective bridging and shell of GQDs.

To better understand the superior electrochemical performances of the NP-SiAl/GQDs composite compared with the NP-SiAl, EIS tests were performed at open-circuit voltage. In Fig. 6b, the semicircle at high-frequency is related to double-layer capacitance and the charge-transfer resistance (Rct) between the electrode and electrolyte, while the slope of the line in low-frequency zone can be used to characterize the diffusion rate of Li+ [49,50]. Here, we employed a simple equivalent circuit embedded in Fig. 6b to model the sole semicircle behaviors. The corresponding equivalent circuit diagram includes the transfer resistance of lithium ions in the electrolyte (Re), charge transfer resistance (Rct), charge transfer capacitance (CPE), and diffusion impedance (W) [2,32]. The fitting line and fitted data were shown in Fig. S3 and Table S1, respectively. It is obvious that the Rct of NP-SiAl/GQDs sample (143.1 Ω) is much lower than that of NP-SiAl (512.8 Ω). The result demonstrates that the introduction of GQDs can greatly improve the electronic conductivity of SiAl. In addition, the slope of the low-frequency line is related to the lithiumion transfer kinetics within the electrodes [43]. It is noted that the low-frequency line of NP-SiAl/GQDs is much steeper than that of NP-SiAl, indicating better lithium intercalation kinetics in NP-SiAl/GQDs electrode. These results demonstrate that the GQDs can significantly accelerate the electron transfer rate, leading to the large enhancement in the lithiation/delithiation rate. The experimental observations indicate that as-made NP-SiAl/GQDs anode shows excellent electrochemical performances including superior reversible capacity, good rate capability, as well as long and stable cycle life. It is considered that the GQDs and the residual Al dispersoid can offer improved electrical conductivity for NP-SiAl anode. In addition, the porous architecture can provide ample buffer space to the structure expansion of Si. Especially, the GQDs enwrapped on porous surface can effectively avoid the Si aggregation during the lithiation/delithiation process as well as reduce the fracture of Si to tiny particles during the repeated volume expansion and shrinkage process.

In summary, nanoporous SiAl with the encapsulation of graphene quantum dots was easily prepared based on the strategy of facile dealloying and the electrostatic assembly. NP-SiAl/GQDs composite exhibits high reversible capacity and superior cycling performances toward lithium storage. The porous nanoscale architecture created by the dealloying way can provide sufficient space for volume expansion for Si anode during charge/discharge process. The in-situ enwrapping of GQDs on nanoporous surface can not only delay the structure destroy but also provide smooth channels for ions and electrons transportation, resulting in dramatically improved reversible capacity and cycling stability. The as-made NP-SiAl/GQDs established promising application prospects as next-generation anode in lithium ion batteries.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51772133), Shandong Province (ZR2017JL022), the program for Taishan Scholar of Shandong province (ts201712048), and China postdoctoral science foundation (2017M622117).

ce:section.ce:para:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.electacta.2019.06.071.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Porous silicon in carbon cages as high-performance lithium-ion battery anode materials
Electrochim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.G. Zhang, N. Du, S.J. Zhu, Y.F. Chen, Y.F. Lin. S.L. Wu, D.R. Yang, Porous silicon in carbon cages as high-performance lithium-ion battery anode materials, Electrochim. Acta. 252 (2017) 438-445.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phenylalanine-functionalized graphene quantum dot-silicon nanoparticle composite as an anode material for lithium ion batteries with largely enhanced electrochemical performance
Electrochim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.J. Kong, Y.Q. Yang, R.Y. Li, Z.J. Li, Phenylalanine-functionalized graphene quantum dot-silicon nanoparticle composite as an anode material for lithium ion batteries with largely enhanced electrochemical performance, Electrochim. Acta. 198 (2016) 144-155.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Encapsulation of lithium vanadium phosphate in reduced graphene oxide for a lithium-ion battery cathode with stable elevated temperature performance
Electrochim. Acta

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.H. Lim, Y.H. Jung, S.J. Yeom, H.W. Lee, D.K. Kim, Encapsulation of lithium vanadium phosphate in reduced graphene oxide for a lithium-ion battery cathode with stable elevated temperature performance, Electrochim. Acta. 253 (2017) 208-217.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Silicon based lithium-ion battery anodes: A chronicle perspective review
